Policy language on this driver — CHANGED since the previous statement
  - Inflation has continued to run below the Committee's longer-run objective, partly reflecting declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
  - Market-based measures of inflation compensation moved slightly lower; survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations have remained stable.
  - Inflation is anticipated to remain near its recent low level in the near term but the Committee expects inflation to rise gradually toward 2 percent over the medium term as the labor market improves further and the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate.
  - To support continued progress toward maximum employment and price stability, the Committee today reaffirmed its view that the current 0 to 1/4 percent target range for the federal funds rate remains appropriate.
  - In determining whether it will be appropriate to raise the target range at its next meeting, the Committee will assess progress--both realized and expected--toward its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  - The Committee anticipates that it will be appropriate to raise the target range for the federal funds rate when it has seen some further improvement in the labor market and is reasonably confident that inflation will move back to its 2 percent objective over the medium term.
  - When the Committee decides to begin to remove policy accommodation, it will take a balanced approach consistent with its longer-run goals of maximum employment and inflation of 2 percent.
  - The Committee currently anticipates that, even after employment and inflation are near mandate-consistent levels, economic conditions may, for some time, warrant keeping the target federal funds rate below levels the Committee views as normal in the longer run.
  + Inflation has continued to run below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.

  + Inflation is expected to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.

Core PCE tells a very different, more stable story: 1.19% y/y, essentially flat over three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp), sitting 0.81pp below target with core_3m_annualized only modestly positive at 0.83%. Given negative recent monthly prints and a decelerating momentum trend against a rising comparison base, I expect the next headline y/y print to be flat-to-slightly-down rather than continuing its recent sharp climb.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_mom", "headline_3m_annualized", "mom_gap_vs_outgoing", "core_pce_yoy", "core_yoy_change_3m", "core_3m_annualized"], "falsifier": "A strong positive monthly headline CPI print (>0.2% m/m) driven by broad-based (not just energy) gains would indicate genuine reacceleration, changing this call.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.4}

view.reasoning: Last month's release (headline 1.24% y/y, up sharply from 0.64%) confirmed the acceleration I expected: the prior 0.44→0.64→1.24 sequence reflects energy base effects rolling off, not a genuine reheating. The most recent monthly print (mom -0.05%) actually turned negative, well below the 3-month average of -0.01% and sharply below the outgoing +0.25%, so headline momentum has clearly stalled even as the y/y rate sits at a 12-month high. That yoy jump is a pure base-effect artifact: headline_3m_annualized is -0.14%, meaning if recent flat/negative monthly prints persist, y/y could plateau or even dip slightly next month once compared against a rising year-ago base. Core PCE tells a very different, more stable story: 1.19% y/y, essentially flat over three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp), sitting 0.81pp below target with core_3m_annualized only modestly positive at 0.83%. There is no core acceleration to validate the headline's y/y surge. Policy language corroborates this, attributing the shortfall to energy/import price effects expected to be transitory, with inflation compensation measures still soft. Given negative recent monthly prints and a decelerating momentum trend against a rising comparison base, I expect the next headline y/y print to be flat-to-slightly-down rather than continuing its recent sharp climb.
view.report: Last month's release (headline 1.24% y/y, up sharply from 0.64%) confirmed the acceleration I expected: the prior 0.44→0.64→1.24 sequence reflects energy base effects rolling off, not a genuine reheating. The most recent monthly print (mom -0.05%) actually turned negative, well below the 3-month average of -0.01% and sharply below the outgoing +0.25%, so headline momentum has clearly stalled even as the y/y rate sits at a 12-month high. That yoy jump is a pure base-effect artifact: headline_3m_annualized is -0.14%, meaning if recent flat/negative monthly prints persist, y/y could plateau or even dip slightly next month once compared against a rising year-ago base. Core PCE tells a very different, more stable story: 1.19% y/y, essentially flat over three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp), sitting 0.81pp below target with core_3m_annualized only modestly positive at 0.83%. There is no core acceleration to validate the headline's y/y surge. Policy language corroborates this, attributing the shortfall to energy/import price effects expected to be transitory, with inflation compensation measures still soft. Given negative recent monthly prints and a decelerating momentum trend against a rising comparison base, I expect the next headline y/y print to be flat-to-slightly-down rather than continuing its recent sharp climb.
view.falsifier: A strong positive monthly headline CPI print (>0.2% m/m) driven by broad-based (not just energy) gains would indicate genuine reacceleration, changing this call.

raw_response: {"report": "My prior \"flat\" call was wrong: headline y/y jumped from 0.44 to 0.64 to 1.24 before pulling back to 0.85 last print. The most recent monthly readings (-0.11, -0.05, -0.13) show three consecutive negative m/m prints, and the 3m annualized headline pace is now -1.14%, well below the outgoing 0.27% m/m pace \u2014 a sharp deceleration in momentum even though the y/y rate remains elevated relative to its 12-month range (position 0.71). This looks like energy-driven volatility: the y/y spike to 1.24 was likely a base-effect/energy pop that is now unwinding, consistent with headline falling back to 0.85 despite core staying subdued. Core PCE, the better persistence read, ticked up from ~1.19 to 1.41 y/y \u2014 a genuine but modest increase, and core_3m_annualized (+1.40%) is running close to that y/y level, suggesting no strong core acceleration, just noise around a low, sub-target trend. Policy language reinforces this: inflation compensation \"declined further,\" expectations \"little changed,\" and inflation still expected to \"remain low in the near term.\" Given three straight negative headline monthly prints and negative 3m annualized headline momentum, the more likely near-term path is headline cooling further from 0.85, though the recent volatility in this series limits confidence.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_mom", "headline_3m_annualized", "mom_gap_vs_outgoing", "core_pce_yoy", "core_3m_annualized", "yoy_range_position"], "falsifier": "A strong positive m/m headline CPI print (>0.2%) driven by broad-based, non-energy gains would signal reacceleration and reverse this call.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "inflation", "why": "Energy/food component breakdown would confirm whether recent y/y volatility is purely commodity-driven or broader."}], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.4}

view.reasoning: My prior "flat" call was wrong: headline y/y jumped from 0.44 to 0.64 to 1.24 before pulling back to 0.85 last print. The most recent monthly readings (-0.11, -0.05, -0.13) show three consecutive negative m/m prints, and the 3m annualized headline pace is now -1.14%, well below the outgoing 0.27% m/m pace — a sharp deceleration in momentum even though the y/y rate remains elevated relative to its 12-month range (position 0.71). This looks like energy-driven volatility: the y/y spike to 1.24 was likely a base-effect/energy pop that is now unwinding, consistent with headline falling back to 0.85 despite core staying subdued. Core PCE, the better persistence read, ticked up from ~1.19 to 1.41 y/y — a genuine but modest increase, and core_3m_annualized (+1.40%) is running close to that y/y level, suggesting no strong core acceleration, just noise around a low, sub-target trend. Policy language reinforces this: inflation compensation "declined further," expectations "little changed," and inflation still expected to "remain low in the near term." Given three straight negative headline monthly prints and negative 3m annualized headline momentum, the more likely near-term path is headline cooling further from 0.85, though the recent volatility in this series limits confidence.
view.report: My prior "flat" call was wrong: headline y/y jumped from 0.44 to 0.64 to 1.24 before pulling back to 0.85 last print. The most recent monthly readings (-0.11, -0.05, -0.13) show three consecutive negative m/m prints, and the 3m annualized headline pace is now -1.14%, well below the outgoing 0.27% m/m pace — a sharp deceleration in momentum even though the y/y rate remains elevated relative to its 12-month range (position 0.71). This looks like energy-driven volatility: the y/y spike to 1.24 was likely a base-effect/energy pop that is now unwinding, consistent with headline falling back to 0.85 despite core staying subdued. Core PCE, the better persistence read, ticked up from ~1.19 to 1.41 y/y — a genuine but modest increase, and core_3m_annualized (+1.40%) is running close to that y/y level, suggesting no strong core acceleration, just noise around a low, sub-target trend. Policy language reinforces this: inflation compensation "declined further," expectations "little changed," and inflation still expected to "remain low in the near term." Given three straight negative headline monthly prints and negative 3m annualized headline momentum, the more likely near-term path is headline cooling further from 0.85, though the recent volatility in this series limits confidence.
view.falsifier: A strong positive m/m headline CPI print (>0.2%) driven by broad-based, non-energy gains would signal reacceleration and reverse this call.

raw_response: {"report": "My prior \"down\" call was wrong: headline m/m came in at +0.31%, the strongest print in the 13-month window, and headline y/y ticked up to 0.89% from 0.85%, continuing a multi-month climb from deeply negative territory a year ago. This is exactly the reacceleration scenario I flagged as my falsifier. Core PCE also rose to 1.47% y/y, its second consecutive notable jump (1.19\u21921.41\u21921.47), and core_yoy_change_3m is now +0.27pp, confirming the move isn't just a one-off energy blip in headline. Headline_3m_annualized is soft at 0.54%, but that's dragged down by weak prints two-three months ago; the trajectory (outgoing_mom 0.10, latest 0.31) points up, not down. Base effects are also favorable for continued y/y increases given the low prior-year comparison base. Policy language still describes inflation as running below target with expectations depressed, but that language is unchanged/lagging relative to the recent acceleration in both core and headline readings. Given the strong current m/m print, rising core trend, and low base effects still working through, I expect headline y/y to rise further at the next release rather than fall back.", "key_evidence": "headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m", "falsifier": "A weak m/m headline print (<0.05%) or a pullback in core PCE below 1.3% y/y would suggest the recent acceleration was temporary.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "up", "conviction": 0.55}

view.reasoning: My prior "down" call was wrong: headline m/m came in at +0.31%, the strongest print in the 13-month window, and headline y/y ticked up to 0.89% from 0.85%, continuing a multi-month climb from deeply negative territory a year ago. This is exactly the reacceleration scenario I flagged as my falsifier. Core PCE also rose to 1.47% y/y, its second consecutive notable jump (1.19→1.41→1.47), and core_yoy_change_3m is now +0.27pp, confirming the move isn't just a one-off energy blip in headline. Headline_3m_annualized is soft at 0.54%, but that's dragged down by weak prints two-three months ago; the trajectory (outgoing_mom 0.10, latest 0.31) points up, not down. Base effects are also favorable for continued y/y increases given the low prior-year comparison base. Policy language still describes inflation as running below target with expectations depressed, but that language is unchanged/lagging relative to the recent acceleration in both core and headline readings. Momentum indicators remain constructive: the 3-month annualized headline pace (3.65%) is well above the trailing y/y (1.08%), and the recent mom prints (0.31, 0.38, 0.24, 0.28) are firmer than the mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.14pp) and the prior soft patch (-0.13 to -0.05). Policy language still frames inflation as "below" target and expected to "remain low in the near term," but this is backward-looking boilerplate relative to the acceleration already visible in the data; the only edit was inflation compensation "declined" further, which is a market-based expectations signal not directly about realized inflation. With headline sitting on a plateau after a strong run-up and core still rising, I lean toward continued firmness or modest further increase in headline, though the flat outgoing print tempers conviction. I don't see clear deceleration signals (no soft mom prints, no core pullback below 1.40%).

I weight core PCE for persistence, but headline's sharp yoy drop reinforces rather than contradicts this. I expect the next headline print to continue drifting down or hold near current lows.

view.reasoning: The previous "down" call was directionally right: headline CPI yoy fell from 1.86 to 1.64 before ticking up to 1.73 in the latest print, and core PCE eased from 1.68 to 1.55 before a small bounce to 1.58. view.reasoning: Last release: headline CPI yoy jumped to 1.93% from 1.73%, driven by a strong 0.38% m/m print — well above the prior 3m average of 0.16%. That confirms the "up" call from last time, though the magnitude (biggest single monthly print in the series) is notable. However, core PCE yoy continued to soften, falling to 1.50% from 1.58%, marking a new cycle low and hitting the exact threshold I flagged as a reversal trigger. Core 3m annualized is just 1.16%, well below target-consistent pace, and core_yoy_change_3m is -0.13pp — decelerating even as headline accelerated. This divergence is the key tension: headline's pop looks driven by volatile components (energy/food per mandate), while core — the better persistence signal — is still decelerating and now sits 0.50pp below target. Policy language also shifted to explicitly flag both headline and core running below 2%, reinforcing that the underlying trend is soft. Given one m/m headline print (0.38%) is not yet a trend, and base effects from a strong month will roll off, I lean toward the headline yoy retreating or flattening next release rather than extending the jump, unless energy/food prices stay elevated. I weight core's soft momentum more heavily for the forward read on headline's next move, expecting mean reversion.

The core-minus-headline gap has widened to 0.55pp, confirming the headline move is a base/energy effect rather than broad disinflation. For the next CPI print specifically, headline momentum has been weak-to-negative for three straight months (mom_gap_vs_outgoing -0.30pp), and there's no sign of a rebound in the trend. Given the persistent negative-to-flat mom prints and no evidence of core reaccelerating enough to pull headline up, I lean toward continued flat-to-down headline yoy next month, though the streak of consecutive weak prints raises some risk of mean-reversion/base-effect payback.

Headline 3m annualized has collapsed to -0.85%, and yoy_range_position sits at the bottom of the 12-month range, confirming headline is now decelerating hard. This move looks energy/food driven given the divergence from core: core PCE y/y actually ticked up to 1.70% (from 1.59%), and core 3m annualized is running hot at +2.40%, suggesting underlying persistence is not decelerating — if anything mildly firming. Policy language also flags "muted inflation pressures" and declining market-based inflation compensation, consistent with soft realized inflation and expectations, reinforcing disinflationary risk near-term but not a broad collapse.

Given the mandate to weight core for persistence but headline is what's being forecast here (headline_cpi_yoy), the -0.45% monthly print is likely a one-off (energy/food shock) unlikely to repeat at that magnitude, but the yoy comparison base effect means next month's yoy could stabilize or tick down modestly further depending on the base a year ago. With core diverging upward, I lean toward a smaller further decline or flattening rather than continued sharp deceleration.

Given the falsifier I set (mom rebound above +0.20% with core PCE holding above 1.65%) was clearly not met \u2014 mom is deeply negative and core PCE actually dropped to 1.53%, its distance from target widening to -0.47pp \u2014 this looks like a genuine, severe deceleration event, not noise. However, the magnitude of the last two mom prints (-0.79% in particular) is extreme and likely reflects a one-off shock (collapsing energy prices) that mechanically depresses y/y for a few more months but is unlikely to repeat at the same intensity. The 3m annualized headline of -4.69% is not sustainable; base effects from this collapse will keep y/y low next month, but the mom drops are too large to compound identically. Core PCE momentum (3m ann +1.19%) is comparatively stable, suggesting underlying persistent inflation hasn't fully collapsed \u2014 the headline move is being amplified by a component shock. Policy language shift toward \"weathered recent events\" corroborates a disruptive, not ordinary, disinflation episode. Net: another modest y/y decline is likely as the shock continues to work through, though the pace of decline should moderate.", "key_evidence": "headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m", "falsifier": "A headline mom print back above 0.00% combined with core PCE stabilizing at/above 1.55% would signal the shock is fading rather than deepening further.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "inflation", "why": "Energy/food component breakdown would clarify if the mom collapse is shock-driven or broad-based, sharpening persistence read."}], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.55}

view.reasoning: The previous "down" call was directionally correct and then some: headline CPI y/y collapsed from 1.49% to 0.31%, driven by two consecutive sharply negative mom prints (-0.45%, -0.79%) that look like a demand/energy shock rather than gradual disinflation. Given the falsifier I set (mom rebound above +0.20% with core PCE holding above 1.65%) was clearly not met — mom is deeply negative and core PCE actually dropped to 1.53%, its distance from target widening to -0.47pp — this looks like a genuine, severe deceleration event, not noise. However, the magnitude of the last two mom prints (-0.79% in particular) is extreme and likely reflects a one-off shock (collapsing energy prices) that mechanically depresses y/y for a few more months but is unlikely to repeat at the same intensity. The 3m annualized headline of -4.69% is not sustainable; base effects from this collapse will keep y/y low next month, but the mom drops are too large to compound identically. Core PCE momentum (3m ann +1.19%) is comparatively stable, suggesting underlying persistent inflation hasn't fully collapsed — the headline move is being amplified by a component shock. Policy language shift toward "weathered recent events" corroborates a disruptive, not ordinary, disinflation episode. Net: another modest y/y decline is likely as the shock continues to work through, though the pace of decline should moderate.
view.report: The previous "down" call was directionally correct and then some: headline CPI y/y collapsed from 1.49% to 0.31%, driven by two consecutive sharply negative mom prints (-0.45%, -0.79%) that look like a demand/energy shock rather than gradual disinflation. Given the falsifier I set (mom rebound above +0.20% with core PCE holding above 1.65%) was clearly not met — mom is deeply negative and core PCE actually dropped to 1.53%, its distance from target widening to -0.47pp — this looks like a genuine, severe deceleration event, not noise. raw_response: {"report": "The previous call for reacceleration was correct and the newest print confirms it strongly. Headline CPI y/y jumped from 1.37 to 1.67 to 2.67 \u2014 a large 1.00pp jump in the latest month alone, and the 3m annualized headline pace is running at 4.53%, well above target and above the trailing 12m trend. Core PCE y/y also rose to 1.71, its highest in the 13-month window, with core 3m annualized at 3.44%, also accelerating. The recent m/m prints (0.34, then 0.52) are firmly positive and above the 3m average (0.37), with the latest print the strongest since the series began recovering \u2014 the opposite of the \"near zero or negative\" condition that would have falsified reacceleration. Policy language changes are cosmetic (invasion→war, added "food") and don't signal a change in the Fed's inflation assessment. Given two months of very soft MoM prints, I lean toward continued softening in headline y/y, but with weaker conviction than last time since a base-effect rebound is possible if energy reverses.

raw_response: {"report": "The prior \"flat\" call is roughly vindicated: headline y/y fell from 8.19 to 7.76 (-0.43pp), a meaningful decline, while the mom_gap condition I set (headline MoM below 0.2%, core PCE under 5.2%) was NOT met \u2014 outgoing MoM was 0.56% and core PCE actually ticked up to 5.61 from 5.36, its highest in the sample. So the disinflation case did not clearly restore, but headline still moved down more than I expected given a sticky core.\n\nLooking at momentum: headline_3m_annualized (4.26%) is now running below the 12m trailing y/y (7.76%), consistent with further y/y deceleration mechanically as high base months roll off. But the latest MoM print (0.56%) reaccelerated from the two prior very-low prints (0.06, 0.42), and core PCE y/y is rising again, its third straight increase from 5.11 to 5.36 to 5.61 \u2014 this is the most hawkish core signal in the whole series and argues persistence, not disintegration, of underlying price pressure. Headline's y/y decline is being driven by base effects and volatile components rather than genuine disinflation, since core is diverging upward. Given the tension \u2014 headline trending down, core accelerating \u2014 I lean toward the base-effect arithmetic continuing to pull headline y/y down modestly next print, but with reduced conviction given core's inflection upward signals underlying stickiness that could stall further headline declines.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "core_pce_yoy", "headline_yoy_change_3m", "core_yoy_change_3m", "headline_3m_annualized", "core_3m_annualized", "outgoing_mom", "headline_mom_3m_avg"], "falsifier": "A rise in headline MoM above 0.8% or core PCE y/y continuing above 5.6% would signal renewed broad-based acceleration, invalidating the down call.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning:
The prior "flat" call is roughly vindicated: headline y/y fell from 8.19 to 7.76 (-0.43pp), a meaningful decline, while the mom_gap condition I set (headline MoM below 0.2%, core PCE under 5.2%) was NOT met — outgoing MoM was 0.56% and core PCE actually ticked up to 5.61 from 5.36, its highest in the sample. So the disinflation case did not clearly restore, but headline still moved down more than I expected given a sticky core.

Looking at momentum: headline_3m_annualized (4.26%) is now running below the 12m trailing y/y (7.76%), consistent with further y/y deceleration mechanically as high base months roll off. But the latest MoM print (0.56%) reaccelerated from the two prior very-low prints (0.06, 0.42), and core PCE y/y is rising again, its third straight increase from 5.11 to 5.36 to 5.61 — this is the most hawkish core signal in the whole series and argues persistence, not disintegration, of underlying price pressure. Headline's y/y decline is being driven by base effects and volatile components rather than genuine disinflation, since core is diverging upward. Given the tension — headline trending down, core accelerating — I lean toward the base-effect arithmetic continuing to pull headline y/y down modestly next print, but with reduced conviction given core's inflection upward signals underlying stickiness that could stall further headline declines.
view.report:
The prior "flat" call is roughly vindicated: headline y/y fell from 8.19 to 7.76 (-0.43pp), a meaningful decline, while the mom_gap condition I set (headline MoM below 0.2%, core PCE under 5.2%) was NOT met — outgoing MoM was 0.56% and core PCE actually ticked up to 5.61 from 5.36, its highest in the sample. So the disinflation case did not clearly restore, but headline still moved down more than I expected given a sticky core.

Looking at momentum: headline_3m_annualized (4.26%) is now running below the 12m trailing y/y (7.76%), consistent with further y/y deceleration mechanically as high base months roll off. But the latest MoM print (0.56%) reaccelerated from the two prior very-low prints (0.06, 0.42), and core PCE y/y is rising again, its third straight increase from 5.11 to 5.36 to 5.61 — this is the most hawkish core signal in the whole series and argues persistence, not disintegration, of underlying price pressure. Headline's y/y decline is being driven by base effects and volatile components rather than genuine disinflation, since core is diverging upward. Given the tension — headline trending down, core accelerating — I lean toward the base-effect arithmetic continuing to pull headline y/y down modestly next print, but with reduced conviction given core's inflection upward signals underlying stickiness that could stall further headline declines.
view.falsifier: A rise in headline MoM above 0.8% or core PCE y/y continuing above 5.6% would signal renewed broad-based acceleration, invalidating the down call.

raw_response: {"report": "The last release actually validated the prior \"down\" call: headline y/y ticked up only marginally (4.92\u21924.95, essentially flat) after a large multi-month decline, and the mom print (0.34%) stayed well below my 0.5% trigger. Base effects have done most of the work \u2014 headline_yoy_change_3m is still -1.38pp and the series sits near its 12-month low (yoy_above_12m_low +0.03pp). However, the momentum underneath is less reassuring: outgoing_mom of 0.95% will roll off the base next month, and headline_mom_3m_avg (0.27%) combined with core_3m_annualized (4.74%) shows sequential price pressure is still running well above target, not decelerating further in the near term. Core PCE y/y has ground down only slowly (5.59\u21924.78) and remains 2.78pp above target, with core_minus_headline now negative \u2014 core is the sticky, persistent read and it is not falling fast. Given that a very favorable base-effect comparison is exhausted, and monthly momentum has stabilized rather than continued falling, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up at the next print rather than resuming its steep drop. This is a shift from my prior stance given the flattening in the yoy series and firming momentum data.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_mom", "core_pce_yoy", "headline_yoy_change_3m", "core_3m_annualized", "headline_mom_3m_avg", "outgoing_mom", "core_distance_from_target"], "falsifier": "A headline MoM print below 0.15% or a fresh drop in core PCE y/y below 4.7% would confirm renewed disinflation and reverse this flat/up lean.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.4}

view.reasoning: The last release actually validated the prior "down" call: headline y/y ticked up only marginally (4.92→4.95, essentially flat) after a large multi-month decline, and the mom print (0.34%) stayed well below my 0.5% trigger. Base effects have done most of the work — headline_yoy_change_3m is still -1.38pp and the series sits near its 12-month low (yoy_above_12m_low +0.03pp). However, the momentum underneath is less reassuring: outgoing_mom of 0.95% will roll off the base next month, and headline_mom_3m_avg (0.27%) combined with core_3m_annualized (4.74%) shows sequential price pressure is still running well above target, not decelerating further in the near term. Core PCE y/y has ground down only slowly (5.59→4.78) and remains 2.78pp above target, with core_minus_headline now negative — core is the sticky, persistent read and it is not falling fast. Given that a very favorable base-effect comparison is exhausted, and monthly momentum has stabilized rather than continued falling, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up at the next print rather than resuming its steep drop. This is a shift from my prior stance given the flattening in the yoy series and firming momentum data.
view.report: The last release actually validated the prior "down" call: headline y/y ticked up only marginally (4.92→4.95, essentially flat) after a large multi-month decline, and the mom print (0.34%) stayed well below my 0.5% trigger. Base effects have done most of the work — headline_yoy_change_3m is still -1.38pp and the series sits near its 12-month low (yoy_above_12m_low +0.03pp). However, the momentum underneath is less reassuring: outgoing_mom of 0.95% will roll off the base next month, and headline_mom_3m_avg (0.27%) combined with core_3m_annualized (4.74%) shows sequential price pressure is still running well above target, not decelerating further in the near term. Core PCE y/y has ground down only slowly (5.59→4.78) and remains 2.78pp above target, with core_minus_headline now negative — core is the sticky, persistent read and it is not falling fast. Given that a very favorable base-effect comparison is exhausted, and monthly momentum has stabilized rather than continued falling, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up at the next print rather than resuming its steep drop. This is a shift from my prior stance given the flattening in the yoy series and firming momentum data.
view.falsifier: A headline MoM print below 0.15% or a fresh drop in core PCE y/y below 4.7% would confirm renewed disinflation and reverse this flat/up lean.

raw_response: {"report": "The last print (4.13% y/y headline, +0.16% m/m) came in below my prior falsifier threshold (0.15% m/m was the bar, and 0.16% is essentially at it), and importantly the y/y series shows a large drop from 4.95% to 4.13% \u2014 a 0.82pp deceleration in one month, driven by base effects as a high year-ago comparison rolled off. My previous flat call was too cautious; the disinflation continued more than I weighted. However, looking ahead one month, base effects matter enormously: the outgoing_mom being dropped from the y/y window was 1.26%, a very high comparison, which is why this print fell so much. The next comparison month is unlikely to be as favorable (headline_mom_3m_avg is 0.21%, not extreme), so the pace of y/y decline should moderate sharply from here \u2014 the easy base-effect gains are largely exhausted. Core PCE is at 4.79%, barely moved (core_yoy_change_3m only -0.15pp) and running 4.25% annualized over 3m, still well above 2% and above the 3m headline pace, indicating underlying persistence. Given headline momentum has stabilized (0.12-0.34-0.16% m/m range) rather than continuing to collapse, I expect next month's y/y print to be roughly flat to modestly higher, not a continuation of the sharp drop.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_mom", "core_pce_yoy", "headline_yoy_change_3m", "core_yoy_change_3m", "core_3m_annualized", "outgoing_mom", "headline_mom_3m_avg"], "falsifier": "A headline m/m print above 0.45% would signal reacceleration; one below 0.05% would signal disinflation still has room, given fading base effects.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The last print (4.13% y/y headline, +0.16% m/m) came in below my prior falsifier threshold (0.15% m/m was the bar, and 0.16% is essentially at it), and importantly the y/y series shows a large drop from 4.95% to 4.13% — a 0.82pp deceleration in one month, driven by base effects as a high year-ago comparison rolled off. My previous flat call was too cautious; the disinflation continued more than I weighted. However, looking ahead one month, base effects matter enormously: the outgoing_mom being dropped from the y/y window was 1.26%, a very high comparison, which is why this print fell so much. The next comparison month is unlikely to be as favorable (headline_mom_3m_avg is 0.21%, not extreme), so the pace of y/y decline should moderate sharply from here — the easy base-effect gains are largely exhausted. Core PCE is at 4.79%, barely moved (core_yoy_change_3m only -0.15pp) and running 4.25% annualized over 3m, still well above 2% and above the 3m headline pace, indicating underlying persistence. Given headline momentum has stabilized (0.12-0.34-0.16% m/m range) rather than continuing to collapse, I expect next month's y/y print to be roughly flat to modestly higher, not a continuation of the sharp drop.
view.report: The last print (4.13% y/y headline, +0.16% m/m) came in below my prior falsifier threshold (0.15% m/m was the bar, and 0.16% is essentially at it), and importantly the y/y series shows a large drop from 4.95% to 4.13% — a 0.82pp deceleration in one month, driven by base effects as a high year-ago comparison rolled off. My previous flat call was too cautious; the disinflation continued more than I weighted. However, looking ahead one month, base effects matter enormously: the outgoing_mom being dropped from the y/y window was 1.26%, a very high comparison, which is why this print fell so much. The next comparison month is unlikely to be as favorable (headline_mom_3m_avg is 0.21%, not extreme), so the pace of y/y decline should moderate sharply from here — the easy base-effect gains are largely exhausted. Core PCE is at 4.79%, barely moved (core_yoy_change_3m only -0.15pp) and running 4.25% annualized over 3m, still well above 2% and above the 3m headline pace, indicating underlying persistence. Given headline momentum has stabilized (0.12-0.34-0.16% m/m range) rather than continuing to collapse, I expect next month's y/y print to be roughly flat to modestly higher, not a continuation of the sharp drop.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print above 0.45% would signal reacceleration; one below 0.05% would signal disinflation still has room, given fading base effects.

raw_response: {"report": "The previous call (flat, 0.35) was wrong in direction of magnitude: headline y/y fell sharply from 4.13 to 3.07, a full percentage point drop, driven mainly by base effects rather than deceleration in monthly momentum. The latest m/m print of 0.22% is within the recent average (0.24% 3m avg) and above my \"disinflation continues\" threshold of 0.05%, so the underlying monthly pace has stabilized rather than kept collapsing. Headline 3m annualized (2.94%) is actually now below core 3m annualized (3.97%), meaning core is running hotter than headline on a recent-months basis, even as core y/y has only inched down (4.79\u21924.73). Core's y/y change over 3m is just -0.12pp, essentially flat, while core remains far from target (+2.73pp) and above headline. Base effects that drove the big headline y/y drop are largely exhausted now (headline_yoy_12m_low just hit at 3.07%, range_position at 0), so next month's y/y is unlikely to fall by another full point \u2014 the mechanical tailwind fades. With m/m running near 0.22-0.24% and less favorable base comparisons ahead, I expect headline y/y to be roughly stable to modestly higher, not a continued sharp decline. Core's stickiness reinforces that underlying inflation isn't decelerating much further, so I lean flat-to-up on headline for the next print.", "key_evidence": "headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, headline_yoy_12m_low", "falsifier": "A headline m/m print below 0.10% would signal base-effect exhaustion is being offset by genuine disinflation; above 0.35% would confirm reacceleration.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The previous call (flat, 0.35) was wrong in direction of magnitude: headline y/y fell sharply from 4.13 to 3.07, a full percentage point drop, driven mainly by base effects rather than deceleration in monthly momentum. The latest m/m print of 0.22% is within the recent average (0.24% 3m avg) and above my "disinflation continues" threshold of 0.05%, so the underlying monthly pace has stabilized rather than kept collapsing. Headline 3m annualized (2.94%) is actually now below core 3m annualized (3.97%), meaning core is running hotter than headline on a recent-months basis, even as core y/y has only inched down (4.79→4.73). Core's y/y change over 3m is just -0.12pp, essentially flat, while core remains far from target (+2.73pp) and above headline. Base effects that drove the big headline y/y drop are largely exhausted now (headline_yoy_12m_low just hit at 3.07%, range_position at 0), so next month's y/y is unlikely to fall by another full point — the mechanical tailwind fades. With m/m running near 0.22-0.24% and less favorable base comparisons ahead, I expect headline y/y to be roughly stable to modestly higher, not a continued sharp decline. Core's stickiness reinforces that underlying inflation isn't decelerating much further, so I lean flat-to-up on headline for the next print.
view.report: The previous call (flat, 0.35) was wrong in direction of magnitude: headline y/y fell sharply from 4.13 to 3.07, a full percentage point drop, driven mainly by base effects rather than deceleration in monthly momentum. The latest m/m print of 0.22% is within the recent average (0.24% 3m avg) and above my "disinflation continues" threshold of 0.05%, so the underlying monthly pace has stabilized rather than kept collapsing. Headline 3m annualized (2.94%) is actually now below core 3m annualized (3.97%), meaning core is running hotter than headline on a recent-months basis, even as core y/y has only inched down (4.79→4.73). Core's y/y change over 3m is just -0.12pp, essentially flat, while core remains far from target (+2.73pp) and above headline. Base effects that drove the big headline y/y drop are largely exhausted now (headline_yoy_12m_low just hit at 3.07%, range_position at 0), so next month's y/y is unlikely to fall by another full point — the mechanical tailwind fades. With m/m running near 0.22-0.24% and less favorable base comparisons ahead, I expect headline y/y to be roughly stable to modestly higher, not a continued sharp decline. Core's stickiness reinforces that underlying inflation isn't decelerating much further, so I lean flat-to-up on headline for the next print.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print below 0.10% would signal base-effect exhaustion is being offset by genuine disinflation; above 0.35% would confirm reacceleration.

Core PCE also ticked up to 2.91% from 2.86%, and its 3m annualized pace (2.97%) is consistent with a core y/y still near 2.9%, well above the 2% target (core_distance_from_target 0.91pp). Core-minus-headline is only -0.11pp, so the two measures are telling a consistent story rather than diverging sharply. Weighting core for persistence, but headline's recent momentum is now the more informative signal for the next print given its faster 3m annualized pace. With outgoing momentum unchanged (mom_gap_vs_outgoing=0.00) and headline sitting at a 12-month high, I see continued mild upward pressure into the next release, though the pace of acceleration itself may be leveling rather than steepening further.

view.reasoning: The previous "flat" call largely held: headline y/y printed 3.00, essentially unchanged from 2.99, and the m/m gap vs. outgoing (-0.06pp) confirms no acceleration surprise. Headline sits at its 12-month high (yoy_range_position 0.97), having climbed steadily from the 2.33 trough, but the pace of increase has clearly plateaued over the last three prints (2.94, 2.99, 3.00) — a flattening curve rather than a new leg up. Momentum measures are mixed: headline_3m_annualized (3.43%) still runs hotter than the y/y print, suggesting some upward drift risk, but core_3m_annualized (2.40%) is decelerating and core y/y has actually ticked down 0.08pp over three months, with the latest print (2.83) below the recent local peak (2.91). Core running below headline (core_minus_headline -0.17pp) and decelerating is the more important signal for persistence, and it argues against imminent reacceleration in headline. Net: headline is near a local plateau at an elevated level (0.8-1.0pp above target on core), with recent momentum roughly flat-to-slightly-up on headline but cooling on core. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional break either way — most likely essentially unchanged next print, perhaps a marginal uptick given the 3m annualized skew.
view.report: The previous "flat" call largely held: headline y/y printed 3.00, essentially unchanged from 2.99, and the m/m gap vs. outgoing (-0.06pp) confirms no acceleration surprise. Headline sits at its 12-month high (yoy_range_position 0.97), having climbed steadily from the 2.33 trough, but the pace of increase has clearly plateaued over the last three prints (2.94, 2.99, 3.00) — a flattening curve rather than a new leg up. Momentum measures are mixed: headline_3m_annualized (3.43%) still runs hotter than the y/y print, suggesting some upward drift risk, but core_3m_annualized (2.40%) is decelerating and core y/y has actually ticked down 0.08pp over three months, with the latest print (2.83) below the recent local peak (2.91). Core running below headline (core_minus_headline -0.17pp) and decelerating is the more important signal for persistence, and it argues against imminent reacceleration in headline. Net: headline is near a local plateau at an elevated level (0.8-1.0pp above target on core), with recent momentum roughly flat-to-slightly-up on headline but cooling on core. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional break either way — most likely essentially unchanged next print, perhaps a marginal uptick given the 3m annualized skew.
